name: Chicken Thigh

identity.basisReason: Raw per-100g meat-only basis is the cleanest base-ingredient record and avoids cooking-fat or skin-on variability.
includes: plain raw chicken thigh meat without breading

metricProvenance.header: USDA FoodData Central style raw chicken-thigh meat-only entry, normalized to per 100 g edible portion
metricProvenance.saturated_fat_g: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.omega3_mg: derived fatty-acid field from the same source family; acceptable as supporting data but not a key meat-scoring fact
metricProvenance.polyunsaturated_fat_g: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.cholesterol_mg: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.collagen_g: withheld, exact cut-specific value not locked from a canonical source
metricProvenance.essential_amino_acids_score: withheld, proxy score removed until exact amino-acid-source backing is attached
metricProvenance.nonessential_amino_acids_score: withheld, proxy score removed until exact amino-acid-source backing is attached
metricProvenance.bioavailability_percent: withheld, protein quality is directionally true but the percent figure was still a proxy
metricProvenance.vitamin_a_dv: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.vitamin_c_dv: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.vitamin_d_dv: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.vitamin_e_dv: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.vitamin_k_dv: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.vitamin_b12_dv: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.iron_dv: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.magnesium_dv: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.zinc_dv: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.calcium_dv: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.potassium_dv: same canonical source family as header

pros.title: dark meat stays flavorful and forgiving to cook
pros.explanation: That makes chicken thigh easier to use well in real meals than drier leaner cuts.

pros.title: usually a reasonably affordable whole-food protein
pros.explanation: Chicken thigh is often cheaper than premium animal proteins while still providing a substantial protein serving.

pros.title: easy to build complete meals around
pros.explanation: It works as an actual centrepiece protein rather than a garnish ingredient.

cons.title: fattier than lean poultry options like breast meat
cons.explanation: That tradeoff is part of why thigh tastes better but is not the cleanest poultry profile.

cons.title: the final health profile changes a lot with cooking method
cons.explanation: Breading, deep frying, or skin-on preparation can shift the outcome substantially from this base record.

cons.title: raw poultry needs careful handling
cons.explanation: Food-safety friction is a practical downside versus foods that are safer or easier to store and prep.

scoreReadiness.summary: Identity and core raw nutrient fields are much cleaner now, but the meats ruleset still leans on removed protein-quality proxy metrics for polished scoring output.
blockers: meats ruleset still expects amino-acid and bioavailability proxy fields that were not honest enough to keep
would benefit from exact source record attachment for the chosen skinless meat-only raw form

sourceNotes: Identity locked to chicken thigh, meat only, raw, per 100 g edible portion.
Core nutrient values were tightened toward common USDA FoodData Central style raw chicken thigh meat-only entries.
Weak proxy metrics were removed rather than preserved as fake certainty.
